coredata.dc:title: Laponite-activated AIE supramolecular assembly with modulating multicolor luminescence for logic digital encryption and perfluorinated pollutant detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Wei-Lei
Wu, Yun-Ga
Wang, Siwei
Zhang, Rong
Wang, Li-Hua
Liu, Jinglin
Xu, Xiufang
coredata.dc:description: Recently, the non-covalently activated supramolecular scaffold method has become a prominent research area in the field of intelligent materials. Here, the inorganic clay (LP) promoted the AIE properties of 4,4′,4″,4‴-(ethene-1,1,2,2-tetrayltetrakis(benzene-4,1-diyl))tetrakis(1-ethylpyridin-1-ium) (P-TPE), showing an astonishing 42-fold enhancement of the emission intensity of the yellow-green luminescence and a 34-fold increase of the quantum yield via organic-inorganic supramolecular strategy as well as the efficient light-harvesting properties (energy transfer efficiency up to 33 %) after doping with the dye receptor Rhodamine B. Furthermore, the full-color spectral regulation, including white light, was achieved by adjusting the ratio of the donor to the acceptor component and co-assembling with the carbon dots (CD). Interestingly, this TPE-based non-covalently activated full-color supramolecular light-harvesting system (LHS) could be achieved not only in aqueous media but also in the hydrogel and the solid state. More importantly, this panchromatic tunable supramolecular LHS exhibited the multi-mode and quadruple digital logic encryption property as well as the specific detection ability towards the perfluorobutyric acid and the perfluorobutanesulfonic acid, which are harmful to human health in drinking water. This result develops a simple, convenient and effective approach for the intelligent anti-counterfeiting and the pollutant sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Laponite-activated
AIE supramolecular assembly
Multicolor luminescence
Light-harvesting system
Intelligent anti-counterfeiting and sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of the energy transfer efficiency and antenna effect
Preparation of the P-TPE/LP assembly and hydrogel
Preparation and characterization of P-TPE/LP assembly
Logical digital encryption and sensing applications

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22101143 and 22361036), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M691661), the Program for Young Talents of Science and Technology in Universities of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region (NJYT22050) and the Inner Mongolia Minzu University Doctoral Research Fund (BS554).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption spectra of adding LP (from 0 wt% to 3 wt%) to P-TPE (0.01 mM, black) in water at 25 °C; (b) Fluorescence spectra of P-TPE (black) with LP (from 0 wt% to 3 wt%) in water at 25 °C (λex = 365 nm); The fluorescence decay curves of (c) P-TPE and (d) P-TPE/LP (LP = 2 wt%) at 535 nm at 25 °C.

(a) Normalized emission spectrum of P-TPE/LP (LP = 2 wt%); Fluorescence spectrum of P-TPE/LP with different concentrations of RhB (b) in aqueous solution and (e) in hydrogel; (c) The CIE chromaticity diagrams of the luminescence color changes from varying the ratios of RhB in the assembly; (d) Fluorescence spectra of P-TPE (black, 0.01 mM) with LP (6 wt%) in water at 25 °C (λex = 365 nm); (f) Fluorescence spectra of LP (black, 2 wt%) with different concentrations of P-TPE (from 0.001 mM to 0.01 mM) in water at 25 °C.

(a) Excitation and emission spectra of the CD ([CD] = 1 μg mL−1, 25 °C). Inset: corresponding photographic images of CD aqueous solution under 365 nm UV light; Fluorescence spectra of adding CD into P-TPE/LP/RhB assembly (b) in aqueous solution and (d) in hydrogel; CIE chromaticity diagram of corresponding the full-color luminescent coordinates (c) in aqueous solution and (e) in hydrogel; (f) Comparison fluorescence spectra of P-TPE in solution and solid with the same amount of LP ([P-TPE] = 0.001 mM, [LP] = 2 wt% and 6 wt%, RhB = 0.01 mM).

(a) Truth tables of white light output of these combinational logic gate systems based on different assembly inputs (a, b and c represent the white light outputs of the three logic gates); (b) Photographs of multilevel information storage with supramolecular ink under daylight and 365 nm irradiation ([P-TPE] = 0.001 mM, [LP] = 2 wt%, RhB = 0.01 mM, CD = 8.5 μg/mL); Photographs of multilevel (c) hydrogel and (d) solid information with the multicolor assembly on a black substrate ([P-TPE] = 0.001 mM, [LP] = 6 wt%, RhB = 0.01 mM, CD = 8.5 μg/mL); (e) Photographs of multicolor-emitting diode (365 nm excited diode insert into different assembly hydrogel).

(a) Photographs of multicolor LHS assembly for the sensing PFC and PFS under 365 nm irradiation in an aqueous solution; Corresponding luminescent quenching efficiency for the emission of (b) green P-TPE/LP, (c) yellow P-TPE/LP/RhB and (d) white P-TPE/LP/RhB/CD for different pollutants; (e) Diagrams and photographs of yellow luminescent type “1” hydrogel P-TPE/LP/RhB for the detection of PFS and PFC ([P-TPE] = 0.001 mM, [LP] = 6 wt%, RhB = 0.01 mM, CD = 8.5 μg/mL).
The illustration of the construction of the LP-activated full-color supramolecular LHS.

head.ce:title: Laponite-activated AIE supramolecular assembly with modulating multicolor luminescence for logic digital encryption and perfluorinated pollutant detection

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, the non-covalently activated supramolecular scaffold method has become a prominent research area in the field of intelligent materials. Here, the inorganic clay (LP) promoted the AIE properties of 4,4′,4″,4‴-(ethene-1,1,2,2-tetrayltetrakis(benzene-4,1-diyl))tetrakis(1-ethylpyridin-1-ium) (P-TPE), showing an astonishing 42-fold enhancement of the emission intensity of the yellow-green luminescence and a 34-fold increase of the quantum yield via organic-inorganic supramolecular strategy as well as the efficient light-harvesting properties (energy transfer efficiency up to 33 %) after doping with the dye receptor Rhodamine B. Furthermore, the full-color spectral regulation, including white light, was achieved by adjusting the ratio of the donor to the acceptor component and co-assembling with the carbon dots (CD). Interestingly, this TPE-based non-covalently activated full-color supramolecular light-harvesting system (LHS) could be achieved not only in aqueous media but also in the hydrogel and the solid state. More importantly, this panchromatic tunable supramolecular LHS exhibited the multi-mode and quadruple digital logic encryption property as well as the specific detection ability towards the perfluorobutyric acid and the perfluorobutanesulfonic acid, which are harmful to human health in drinking water. This result develops a simple, convenient and effective approach for the intelligent anti-counterfeiting and the pollutant sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The light-harvesting system (LHS), a set of donor-acceptor system capable of converting the light energy to the chemical energy, plays a vital role in the photosynthesis process (Z. Wang et al., 2022; Peng et al., 2015; Kundu and Patra, 2017; Scholes et al., 2011; McDermott et al., 1995; J. Li et al., 2017; X. Wang et al., 2010). Imitating the natural lighting process, the efficient and rapid LHS from the donor to the acceptor provides a new hope for the modern energy crisis which can not only capture the abundant solar energy into the chemical energy and even the clean electric energy but also minimize the energy loss, thus ensuring the sufficient fuel and the production of various compounds (Kundu and Patra, 2017; Scholes et al., 2011; Jia et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023). Meanwhile, the multi-color luminescent LHS has also been widely used in many fields such as biological imaging, optoelectronic device, anti-counterfeiting, photocatalysis, and so on, which has promoted scientists to conduct the in-depth research (Chen et al., 2020; Hao et al., 2020; Hedley et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2022; Y. Wang et al., 2023; W. Li et al., 2021). Therefore, the design of the artificial LHS is particularly important. However, the traditional organic fluorescent molecules will produce the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) once stacked (Jia et al., 2023; Dai et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2020), which greatly limits their applications in LHS. The aggregation-induced emission (AIE), that is, the high emission efficiency in the aggregation state (Biedermann et al., 2012; Luo et al., 2001; Mei et al., 2015; Xiao et al., 2022; Z. Zhang et al., 2022; Q. Zhang et al., 2023; Zuo et al., 2021), especially the common tetraphenylethylene (TPE) derivatives can not only overcome the ACQ problem under stacking conditions, but also has the excellent light stability and biocompatibility as well as the large Stokes shift, which is conducive to the construction of a multi-functional and efficient LHS.

Moreover, supramolecular assemblies based on non-covalent interactions have attracted extensive attentions due to their important applications in fields of LED luminescence, information anti-counterfeiting and biomedical materials (Ding et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2022; Xiong et al., 2023; Wei et al., 2023; Wei et al., 2022; Lv et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2020; Li and Yang, 2021). Taking use of supramolecular non-covalent interactions, including host-guest interactions, multiple hydrogen bonds, electrostatic interactions, π-π stacking and other interactions, to construct the supramolecular LHS based on AIE properties of TPE has become one of hot topics in the current LHS research. Tang prepared a series of novel TPE aggregation-induced luminescent covalent organic cages with the high emission intensity by reducing metal cages (Qin et al., 2023), where the efficient LHS was constructed with the dye Nile red (NIR) for effectively catalyzing the dehalogenation reaction. Wang built a high efficiency and two-step energy transfer supramolecular assembly via the carboxyl pillar[5]arene, the quaternary ammonium salt TPE and dyes Eosin Y (ESY)/NIR, which gave the 96% yield in the dehalogenation reaction of a-bromoacetophenone (Hao et al., 2020). Shi reported a wheel-like compound based on the platinum(II) metal ring and the double TPE-terminated naphthalene to achieve a continuous and efficient LHS with ESY and NIR (Shi et al., 2023). Liu used sulfobutyl β-cyclodextrin and cucurbit[8]uril (CB[8]) to promote the luminescence of tetraphenylethene pyridine and realized the multi-color LHS with the sulfonated aluminum phthalocyanine for the logic gate anti-counterfeiting (Tian et al., 2023). However, the organic-inorganic hydrid TPE supramolecular scaffold with a rigid tissue and AIE properties are still rare. Herein, we used laponite® clay (LP) nanosheets with a negatively charged surface and positively charged edges as an inorganic scaffold (Garain et al., 2021; C. Li et al., 2023) and a TPE derivative (P-TPE) with four cations as luminescent cores to construct a processable multi-color luminescent soft material (Scheme 1 ). The electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged surface of LP and the positively charged P-TPE could limit the free rotation of the P-TPE molecule and reduce the non-radiative transition, leading to a 42-time enhancement of the yellow-green fluorescence intensity and a 34-time enhancement of the yield. Interestingly, the secondary assembly with the dye rhodamine B (RhB) exhibited a significant light-harvesting performance with a high energy transfer efficiency (>33 %). Subsequently, the positively charged edge of LP was further assembled with the negatively charged blue carbon dots (CD) to obtain a full-color luminescence including the white light (CIE 0.31, 0.33) by adjusting the ratio of donor/acceptor to CD. Beside in solution, this LP-promoted TPE multi-level supramolecular LHS could also be directly prepared into a processable hydrogel medium and further used in the solid state for the tunable multilevel output of full-color luminescence that was able to be applied to the multi-level logic gate anti-counterfeit material and the selective and sensitive detection of perfluorobutyric acid (PFC) and perfluorobutanesulfonic acid (PFS) with a simple way for the development of the advanced intelligent system.

All chemicals were commercially available unless noted otherwise. P-TPE was prepared according to the literature procedures (Tian et al., 2023). 1,1,2,2-Tetrakis(4-(pyridin-4-yl)phenyl)ethene was purchased from bidepharm. Bromoethane, 1,2-dibromoethane(DBE), 1-bromododecane(BDD) and 4-bromobutyric acid(BBA) were purchased from Heowns. Perfluorobutyric acid (PFC) and perfluorobutanesulfonic acid (PFS) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. LP was purchased from BYK Additives Inc.

NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker AV500 spectrometer. The steady-state fluorescence data were collected from the Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer and HITACHI fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600S). UV–vis spectra and optical transmittance were recorded in a quartz cell (light path, 10 mm) on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer equipped with a PTC-348WI temperature controller. Photoluminescence spectra and fluorescence lifetimes were measured by means of time–correlated single–photon counting on a FLS1000 instrument (Edinburg Instruments, Livingstone, UK). High–resolution transmission electron microscopy images were acquired using a Tecnai 20 high–resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 keV. The sample was prepared by dropping the solution onto a copper grid, which was then air-dried. The zeta potentials and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were determined on a NanoBrook 90Plus at 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the energy transfer efficiency and antenna effect
ce:section.ce:para:
The energy transfer efficiency (ՓET), the proportion of absorbed energy transferred to the receptor, is a key parameter for determining the practical utility of FRET. It is the ratio of the donor luminescence intensity in the absence and presence of the acceptor (ID and IDA) (according to donor: 535 nm and RhB acceptor: 585 nm) calculated from spectral titration experiments (J. Li et al., 2017).
Փ
E T
= 1 −
I DA
I D

The antenna effect (AE) value is another important index to measure FRET, which represents the ratio of the emission intensity of the donor to the acceptor after the donor is excited at a certain concentration. IA + D (λex = 365 nm) and IA + D (λex = 550 nm) represent the acceptor luminescence intensity of the system under the donor and acceptor excitation conditions, respectively, and ID (λex = 365 nm) represents the emission intensity under the donor excitation without the acceptor.
Antenna
effect =
I
A + D
(
λ = 365
nm
)
−
I
D
(
λ = 365
nm
)
I
A + D
(
λ = 550
nm
)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the P-TPE/LP assembly and hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of P-TPE was dissolve in deionized water, and a certain amount of LP was add to the above solution. The hydrogel was constructed by adding 6 wt% LP to the P-TPE solution followed by the ultrasonic dissolution and the direct coagulation for the later use. The modular gel was prepared by heating the mixture of the P-TPE/LP assembly and agarose at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of P-TPE/LP assembly
ce:section.ce:para: P-TPE was obtained in 93% yield through the reaction of a large excess of bromoethane with 1,1,2,2-tetrakis(4-(pyridin-4-yl)phenyl)ethene (1) in acetonitrile overnight and then filtered by diethyl ether (Figs. S1–S2). Possessing four positive charges, P-TPE is suitable for assembling with the LP nanoclay with the orthogonally charged surface (positive charge) and edge (negative charge). The changes of optical properties after the addition of LP in P-TPE aqueous solution were studied by the UV-vis spectroscopy and the photoluminescence spectroscopy. The UV-vis spectrum of the free P-TPE showed two characteristic absorption peaks at 275 nm and 350 nm. After adding LP to P-TPE, the absorption intensity at 275 nm continuously increased until 2% LP was added (Fig. 1 a). Interestingly, the addition of the LP clay made the fluorescence of P-TPE at 550 nm blue-shifted by 15 nm, accompanied by the fluorescence enhancement. When 2% LP was added, a 42-fold increase of the fluorescence intensity was achieved (Fig. 1b and S3). In addition, the fluorescence lifetime enhanced from 1.7 ns to 34.2 ns (increased by 2.5 times), and the fluorescence quantum yield was enhanced from 1.99% to 68.09% (increased by 34 times) (Fig. 1c and d, S4 and S5). This phenomenon reveals that the formation of supramolecular assembly of P-TPE and LP effectively limits the free rotation of the tetraphenylethylene group and lights up the luminescence of P-TPE. However, continuously adding LP quenched the fluorescence to some extent (Fig. 1b), probably because of the competitive bonding of various charges. The zeta potential of P-TPE was measured to be 6.53 mV, indicating that TPE was positively charged and could be assembled with a negatively charged host. In addition, the zeta potential of the P-TPE/LP assembly increased from −34.30 mV (free LP) to −25.62 mV, indicating that the electrostatic interaction between P-TPE and the negatively charged surface of LP limits the non-radiative transition and improves the luminescence performance (Fig. S6). In the control experiments, the negatively charged hyaluronic acid and sodium carboxymethyl cellulose were added to the aqueous solution of P-TPE, and the fluorescence intensity was slightly enhanced (Figs. S7a–b). In addition, we added the positively charged β-cyclodextrin or chitosan with the same charge as P-TPE before constructing the assembly (Figs. S7c–d). We found that after LP was assembled with cyclodextrin/chitosan and P-TPE successively, the luminescence was greatly quenched, mainly because the negative charge on LP is neutralized by the positive charge of the positively charged β-cyclodextrin or chitosan. This phenomenon further proved that the non-covalent interaction between LP and P-TPE was mainly the electrostatic interaction. TEM images of the P-TPE/LP assembly showed many lamellar aggregates, which are very different from the irregular small nanosheets of P-TPE (Fig. S8). The DLS data also showed that the size of P-TPE and LP increased significantly from 513 nm to 2936 nm after assembly (Fig. S9).

Owing to the excellent fluorescence properties of the P-TPE/LP assembly and the multi-charged scaffold of LP, the P-TPE/LP assembly is suitable as a good artificial light harvesting platform for the energy transfer with the charged dyes. Fig. 2 a gives the normalized diagram of the fluorescence emission of the P-TPE/LP assembly and the UV absorption and the fluorescence emission of the dye rhodamine B (RhB). The fluorescence emission of the P-TPE/LP assembly has a good overlap with the UV-visible absorption of RhB, which is suitable for the energy transfer as a fluorescence donor and RhB as a fluorescence acceptor. After gradually adding RhB to the P-TPE/LP solution, the fluorescence intensity at 535 nm gradually decreases, and the lifetime and the quantum yield decrease to 4.1 ns and 15.02%, respectively (Fig. 2b–S10-S12). However, the fluorescence intensity at 585 nm enhanced with the lifetime increasing by 1.1 times (from 4.4 ns to 5.2 ns) and the quantum yield increasing by 1.2 times (from 42.02% to 54.19%), which proved that the system had a good energy light harvesting property. According to the fluorescence quenching of P-TPE/LP/RhB in the energy transfer process, the energy transfer efficiency and the antenna effect value were calculated to be 33% and 0.57, when the donor/acceptor molar ratio was 1 : 1 (Figs. S13–14). Meanwhile, the fluorescence color changed from yellow-green to orange, and the corresponding CIE coordinates changed from (0.33, 0.55) to (0.45, 0.47) in Fig. 2c. In addition, due to the electrostatic interaction among LP at higher concentrations, the hybrid containing 6 wt% LP forms a gel with a viscosity of 85 mPaS. The assembly changes from the solution to the gel state once the amount of LP reaching 6 wt%, which caused the same blue shift and enhancement of P-TPE fluorescence. The fluorescence at 535 nm enhanced by 11 times, accompanied by the fluorescence lifetime and the fluorescence quantum yield respectively improving by 2.4 times and 30 times (Fig. 2d–S15-S17). Interestingly, the fluorescence energy resonance transfer also occurs after doping RhB in the gel state (the fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield of RhB increased by 1.1 times and 1.2 times), with the energy transfer efficiency as 49% and an antenna effect as 0.57 when the donor/acceptor molar ratio is 1:1 (Fig. 2e–S13b, S14, S18-S20). We also compared the fluorescence emission of three different concentrations of P-TPE/LP (Fig. 2f) and found that the spectrum red-shifted from green to yellow-green as the concentration increased.

It is noteworthy that the multi-color luminescence has wide applications in the lighting and the anti-counterfeiting, especially the white light emission with the color fidelity and the low color distortion (Zhou and Lin et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2023a, b). To obtain the white light emission, the blue light-emitting carbon dots (CD) was doped into the P-TPE/LP/RhB LHS to balance the yellow light of the system. The zeta potential of CD was measured to be −12.26 mV (Fig. S21a), indicating that there are a large number of carboxylic acid groups on the surface of CD, which are suitable for the further co-assembly with the positive charges on the edge of LP. The absorption and the emission spectra of CD are shown in Fig. 3 a, and the bright pure blue light emission was observed around 460 nm under the excitation at 365 nm. In addition, the LP clay enhanced the fluorescence of CD at 460 nm by 1.6-fold and the fluorescence quantum yield by 1.4-fold (Figs S21b, S22-S24). These are beneficial to the construction of a white light system. Fig. 3b shows the fluorescence spectra when CD is gradually added to the P-TPE/LP and the P-TPE/LP/RhB assembly in which P-TPE/LP, P-TPE/LP/RhB and CD can emit the strong yellow-green, orange and blue lights, corresponding to (0.30, 0.53), (0.41, 0.48) and (0.18, 0.16) coordinates in the CIE diagram, respectively. Interestingly, with the gradual addition of CD in the yellow luminescent P-TPE/LP/RhB assembly, the luminescence of the assembly gradually changes from orange to blue and passes through the white light area, corresponding to the CIE coordinate (0.31,0.33). Importantly, the full-color luminescence could be obtained by adjusting the proportions of CD, TPE and RhB in different proportions (Fig. 3c–S25). After gradually doping CD in the P-TPE/LP/RhB hydrogel, the similar full-color luminescence was realized as yellow-green-orange-blue through white light corresponding to the CIE coordinate (0.31, 0.34) (Fig. 3d and e) by adjusting different ratios (Fig. 3e and S26). More interestingly, the fluorescence intensity under 365 nm excitation increased dramatically after drying the hydrogel P-TPE/LP assembly into a solid powder (Fig. 3f), and the fluorescence spectra of the solid powders of P-TPE/LP, P-TPE/LP/RhB, P-TPE/LP/RhB/CD with corresponding CIE coordinates were in the range of yellowish-green point (0.34, 0.50), orange point (0.42, 0.45) and pure white light point (0.32, 0.33) (Fig. S27). This phenomenon demonstrates that LP could activate the AIE property of P-TPE. The increase of P-TPE concentration limits the non-radiative transitions of TPE and thereby promotes the optical property with a gradual redshift of the emission wavelength. Additionally, P-TPE/LP, P-TPE/LP/RhB and P-TPE/LP/RhB/CD assemblies exhibited the satisfactory photostability, and their spectra hardly changed after the natural light irradiation for 72 h and 120 h (Figs. S28–29), which is an important indicator of the luminescent materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Logical digital encryption and sensing applications
ce:section.ce:para: This tunable full-color luminescent assembly could be applied in the multi-level logic gate anti-counterfeiting and sensing systems. The device defines the different components of the assembly as the inputs and defines the white light emission under 365 nm excitation as the outputs (Fig. 4 a). According to the white light output recorded as 1 and the non-output recorded as 0, three INHIBIT logic gate systems with light outputs were designed. It is worth noting that the output 0 and 1 states of the current logic gate represent several different types of supramolecular assemblies. In the logic gate system, when P-TPE/LP/RhB/CD coexist, it is possible to “lock” the white light emission and utilize the CD as a non-gate to “mute” the output signal. Although a simple logic gate is presented by this LP-illuminated multilevel supramolecular assembly, the increase of the input components makes the output information complex, and in particular, there is a great potential for constructing the complex logic security in a simple way. Furthermore, this light-up full-color luminescent supramolecular assembly P-TPE/LP/RhB/CD was successfully applied to the multi-level information encryption. The P-TPE solution was filled into the porous plate in the form of the number “888”, which was encrypted and hardly visible under the 365 nm irradiation. In some porous plate, with the addition of the host molecule LP, the yellowish-green luminescent number “111” could be presented under the 365 nm irradiation, and the bright yellow luminescent number “520” was presented with the addition of the acceptor molecule RhB. When CD was added, the encrypted white luminescent number “888” appeared under the 365 nm illumination (Fig. 4b). We utilized the LP hydrogel to write the colorless and transparent number “8″ on the glass plate. With the incorporation of each component of the assembly, the different digital information of the different luminescent colors was displayed under the 365 nm irradiation. The incorporation of P-TPE into the LP hydrogel could present a yellow-green luminescent number “1”, the continuous incorporation of the fluorescent acceptor molecule RhB exhibited an orange-yellow luminescent number “0”, and the doping of CD could render as an encrypted white luminescent number “8” (Fig. 4c). The solid powder figures after drying hydrogel yielded the different color figure information with the different components of the doped assemblies under the 365 nm irradiation, and the luminescence was more glaring (Fig. 4d). Considering their excellent panchromatic luminescence properties, we successfully applied these supramolecular assemblies to the multicolor LED luminescence. The commercially available diodes (excitation wavelength of 365 nm) were placed into the centrifuge tubes containing the hydrogels as shown in Fig. 4e. When the diodes were powered by the batteries, the diodes emitted the desired bright multicolor lights of the assemblies. It is shown that the constructed LHS-promoted P-TPE luminescence has the excellent display and quadruple information encryption property.

The concentration levels of PFC and PFS in drinking water from the industrial areas and the nearby communities such as the airport often exceed the health advisory level (70 ng L−13). PFC and PFS bind to the proteins and are highly stable, causing the serious hazards include the kidney and liver damage, immunotoxicity, and cancer (Zheng et al., 2019). It is then of great significance to develop simple, cheap and sensitive methods for the detection of PFS and PFC. Therein, the LP-activated TPE LHS could be used for the selective detection of PFS and PFC. As shown in Fig. 5a–d and S30-S31, after adding PFS into P-TPE/LP, P-TPE/LP/RhB or P-TPE/LP/RhB/CD at a low concentration of 12 mM, the quenching efficiency was as high as 58%, 60% and 58%, respectively, which was twice as high as the corresponding values for PFC at 38 mM, while the luminescence of the assembly did not change in the presence of DBE, BDD and BBA. In addition, the assembly became turbid with the addition of PFC, while the addition of PFS solution led to the formation of gel, due to the charge transfer and the electrostatic interaction. These phenomena prove that LHS has a highly selective detection ability for PFS and PFC. Then, this assembly could be further used to form a modular gel with agarose for the detection of PFC and PFS in water, revealing a significant quenching efficiency, as shown in Fig. 5 e. Therefore, the constructed multicolor LHS has a good application prospect in the selective detection of the perfluorinated pollutants.

In this work, we developed a novel LP-activated hierarchical panchromatic LHS assembly based on a supramolecular scaffold strategy combining with P-TPE, LP, RhB and CD, and successfully applied it to the multi-form and full-color intelligent information anti-counterfeiting logic gates and the selective detection of the perfluorinated pollutants. Due to the strong electrostatic interaction, LP promotes the radiative transition of P-TPE, resulting in a surprising 42-fold increase of the P-TPE luminescence, a 34-fold increase of the quantum yield and a 2.5-fold increase of the lifetime. Benefiting from the multivalent supramolecular scaffold structure, PTPE/LP presents the efficient energy transfer (>33 %) with the organic dye RhB, along with the full-color luminescence including the white light by changing the ratio of donor, acceptor and CD, and this property can be achieved not only in solution but also in the gel state and the solid state. This multi-mode LP-promoted hierarchically tunable full-color supramolecular LHS is a new way to construct the multi-functional anti-counterfeiting materials and the selective detection of perfluorinated pollutants. At present, the system has the advantages of simple operation and convenient detection, but in order to improve the detection effect and practical application, a lot of works still need to be done. Firstly, the energy transfer efficiency and antenna effect in the light-harvest need to be further improved. Secondly, the effect and the selectivity of perfluorinated compound detection are also facing the problem of anti-interference, and further studies on the sensing and removal of perfluorinated pollutants are still in progress.

Wei-Lei Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration. Yun-Ga Wu: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Siwei Wang: Software. Rong Zhang: Software. Li-Hua Wang: Validation. Jinglin Liu: Supervision. Xiufang Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22101143 and 22361036), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M691661), the Program for Young Talents of Science and Technology in Universities of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region (NJYT22050) and the Inner Mongolia Minzu University Doctoral Research Fund (BS554).
